Consumer complaint narrative:
To the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau, I am writing to file a formal complaint against Bank of America regarding their recent actions which I believe to violate consumer rights and fair banking practices. 

On XX/XX/2024, I discovered that all of my Bank of America credit card accounts, which held a history of over a decade and significant credit limits, were inexplicably closed without any prior legal notice. Despite the importance and sensitivity of such matters, I was not formally informed through the appropriate channels, such as via mail, as legally required. Instead, I received a phone call from an undisclosed number, which my XXXX marked as spam or scam. In today 's climate of rampant phishing scams and cybercriminal activity, it is unacceptable for a reputable institution like Bank of America to rely solely on phone calls, especially when they can easily be mistaken for fraudulent attempts. 

When I attempted to verify the authenticity of the call, I was advised by Bank of America XXXX to " just XXXX it '' to confirm whether the call was legitimate or not. Even though I google it, the number was marked as spam in some forums. This XXXX and XXXX approach to customer security is concerning, particularly when dealing with sensitive financial matters. Moreover, it is unreasonable to expect customers to discern between genuine bank communications and potential scams, especially when legitimate calls are flagged as spam by both phones and online resources. 

The abrupt closure of my credit card accounts not only disrupted my financial stability but also had cascading effects on my credit and banking activities. Not only my credit cards though! Without warning, my checking account ending in XXXX was blocked, rendering me unable to pay essential bills such as my mortgage and other credit obligations. This lack of access to my funds caused undue stress and inconvenience, not only for myself but also for my spouse, who shares a mutual account with me. His autopayments were blocked too! 

Furthermore, when I contacted Bank of America to rectify the situation, I was met with further incompetence and disregard for protocol. Instead of addressing the issue promptly and efficiently, I was asked to verbally confirm MY NAME and ADDRESS ONLY! This was their compliance requesta request that is both redundant and insulting given that I have received statements from Bank of America regularly for decades, all of which are addressed to my correct name and address. 

I believe that Bank of America 's actions in this matter are in direct violation of consumer rights as outlined by the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau. As a loyal customer who has entrusted Bank of America with my financial affairs for over a decade, I expect to be treated with the respect and professionalism that such a relationship warrants. Anything less is unacceptable and undermines the trust and confidence I have placed in the banking system. 

I kindly request the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau to investigate this matter thoroughly and take appropriate action to ensure that Bank of America adheres to fair banking practices and provides adequate redress for the harm caused to my credit and XXXX XXXX. 

Thank you for your immediate attention to this matter. I look forward to a favorable resolution. 

Sincerely,

Product: Credit card or prepaid card
Issue: Problem getting a card or closing an account
Consumer complaint narrative:
Netspend Fraud - XX/XX/2020 ( XXXX XXXX ) I believe I am a victim of fraud by Netspend or by people creating fraudulent Netspend credit card accounts under my name. I need help resolving this issue as all my attempts to do so through the company have been unsuccessful. Please provide guidance to me. 

On XX/XX/2020, I got four emails in a row starting around XXXX ET, over the course of 5 minutes, from noreply@netspend.com. The emails were NOT encrypted ( seems very unusual for such emails ) and each contained a security code, like someone was trying to reset a password or enter an account fraudulently. I had previously never heard of Netspend. 

On XX/XX/2020, I went online ( https : //www.netspend.com/help/contact/ ) and called Netspend to look into the situation and try to put an end to any fraudulent activity, which I was very concerned about. Before being able to talk with a customer service representative, the automated phone options ( XXXX ) force you to enter either a Netspend account number or your full social security number. I dont have any account with Netspend and I was calling to close any possible fraudulent account, and there was no way I was going to give over my full social security number given my concerns. 

Unable to reach a customer service representative on the phone, I emailed one of Netspends supposed fraud complaint emails ( spoof@netspend.com ) writing them : I never applied for this card, do not want a card, and believe this represents fraud. It needs to be investigated and the account closed immediately.

I tried calling your phone number, but there is no way to reach someone if you don't have an account ( or maybe even if you do ). Unbelievable.

There was no response from Netspend. 

On XX/XX/2020, I got an email at XXXX XXXX ET from card@email.netspend.com stating that Your Netspend card is on the way, although I never requested a card nor would I ever request one with Netspend. The email stated that my Netspend Visa Prepaid Card has been shipped and is on its way to you. Once your card arrives, be sure to activate it and verify your identity. * After that, add money to your Card Account and then use your card to buy groceries, pay bills,1 and shop online! 

I got similar emails from Netspend on XX/XX/XXXX, XX/XX/XXXX, and XX/XX/XXXX. On XX/XX/XXXX, I received an email from Netspend stating : thank you for requesting your new Netspend Visa Prepaid Card. Don't forget to activate your Card Account and verify your identity! 

I noticed that the different emails from Netspend included information stating my email address ( although written differently than I typically type it ) as well as the last digits of the supposed prepaid card. However, some emails included four digits, while other emails included just three digits. 

On the morning of XX/XX/XXXX, I spent an hour on the phone between XXXX and XXXX trying to reach a customer representative at the customer service number. I entered digits for a social security code ( although I made them up ) when faced with the automated phone system requiring a social security number. Then, after waiting for 10 minutes, I finally reached a representative who immediate started saying she couldnt hear me. I said I could hear her and that I was calling to look into a problem, but she continued to talk over me and then returned me to the automated phone system again.

I called back and used the same method to get to another representative. Again, they asked how they could help me, then subsequently said, If you can hear me caller, please respond. I said I could hear him and asked if he could hear me and that I was calling to report a problem, but he kept repeating the same thing over and over several times, like from a script. And then the line went dead and I was in silence. 

Both interactions seemed very contrived. I believe they could, in fact, hear me and that his was just part of the scam. I have never had any audio issue with my phone and successfully made and received calls with other callers the same day without any audio issues. 

After my frustrating phone calls with Netspend, I then wrote an email at XXXX on XX/XX/XXXX to the XXXX  of Netspend ( XXXX ), and email address that is posted at https : //www.netspend.com/help/contact/ for complaints or compliments. In my email I wrote again that I had never applied for a card, did not want a card, and that I felt this represented fraud and that it needed to be investigated and the account closed immediately. I reported that I had tried to call severals times, but that I had encountered the problems described above with the automated phone system asking for impossible or sensitive information, and then representatives who appeared to be pretending not to hear me while reading from a script. At the end of my email, I wrote that I planned to submit complaints to the Consumer Protection Bureau, the Better Business Bureau, and perhaps law enforcement.

I received an automated reply from donotreply@netspend.com at XXXX stating : Thank you for contacting the Netspend Corporate Office . Your concern is important to us and one of our analysts will respond to you within three business days.

We are closely monitoring the latest news about the current global emergency. We are open, but are currently experiencing extended wait times to speak with our customer service agents. We strongly encourage you to use our digital tools such as your Netspend Mobile App and website ( located on the back of your card ) for self-service banking and convenient account access. 

Additionally, to help ensure you receive the best service experience during this time, our Customer Service hours have temporarily changed to Monday through Friday XXXX CST and Saturday through Sunday XXXX CST. 

Thank you, Netspend Corporate Office I highly doubt that I will receive any further response. The whole situation has been outrageous.
Company public response: Company has responded to the consumer and the CFPB and chooses not to provide a public response
company_behavior_flags: Contrived Interaction|Automated Response Loop|Account Creation Without Consent

Product: Money transfer, virtual currency, or money service
Consumer complaint narrative:
This is to complain against the Binance.US and Binance.com. 

URGENCY : HIGH IMPORTANCE : HIGH [ WITHOUT PREJUDICE ] I would like to draw your attention to a fraudulent crypto scam that occurred between XXXX and XXXX of 2021 - I had sent my complaint electronically to Binance.com, and also have the FBI formally engaged in a case whereby I lost over $ XXXX USD, in which I clearly stated how this scam has affected me personally, psychologically and financially and via a crypto trace, have proven conclusively that the scammers wallet where my funds are located is clearly on your platform and I seek immediate restitution. 

I am afraid I have had to go through so much " bureaucracy '' thus far in order to catch their attention to my concerns. This really doesn't show their complaints department in a good light, to say the least ; and certainly, does not contribute to my overall satisfaction and peace of mind. My complaint is against the Binance.US and Binance.com that did not do its job properly ( could not prevent/foreseen fraud and could not conduct a proper investigation ) and not against the vulnerable customer who fell victim and lost all the savings due to the misconduct of your company. 

I feel very distressed and cheated, all because no one acted immediately and practiced their duty of care, therefore I only request what I believe to be rightfully mine, as all institutions were more than negligent in protecting my account and handling the complaints. I comprehensively provided explanations and proof to my claim, even so, neither Binance.US nor Binance.com never acknowledge my complaint, therefore, I have approached you CFPB and I would like to receive your assistance on this matter. 

General Obligation : Commencing on or about XX/XX/2021, I fell victim to a multi-layered scam operation run by XXXX  Platform which involved me making deposits for a total amount of {$850000.00} USD from my XXXX XXXX and XXXX account to fraudulent investment firm ( s ). From there, the funds were stolen and transferred to your platform to the scammers wallet ( see attached crypto trace ). 

When determining whats reasonable and fair, we should focus on the issue of liability ; common queries include, but are not limited to, the following ( i ) whether Binance.US and Binance.com did not take notice of any rule, law, or regulation, and/or possibly missed any material elements of the relevant bylaws or codes of conduct, that may have prevented them from protecting my financial safety ; ( ii ) whether by virtue of Binance.coms custodianship over my funds or by its control over them, they owed a fiduciary duty to the me and if so, whether that duty was breached ; ( iii ) whether Binance.com promoted the transaction ( s ) in question despite being aware of the nature of the transaction ( s ) in question ( iv ) whether Binance.com was in compliance with its own policies and procedures ; ( v ) whether Binance.com owed duties to myself, what the scope of those duties was, and whether Binance.com did not uphold those duties ; ( vi ) whether Binance.coms conduct was unfair ; and ( vii ) whether Binance.com has within its power the ability to, and should, compensate me for the harm that has befallen me. 

Upon identification of such unusual or suspicious activity, it is crucial that the relevant staff member adequately describe the factors making an activity or transaction suspicious, thoroughly depict the extent and nature of this activity and properly communicate to the customer that such activity meets the relevant criteria of fraud. 

In providing its services to a customer, a financial institution is required by law to exercise the care and skill of a diligent, prudent trading platform. In this case, this means that the payment service provider should not turn a blind eye to known facts pointing to a real possibility that their customer is being scammed. In other words, Binance.com must have had special knowledge of what was occurring or been alerted to a real possibility of fraud taking place. The financial institution must have known or reasonably ought to have known that I was dealing with a scammer. 

Granted, there is room for diversity of view insofar as reasonableness is concerned. Indeed, there is a sense in which the standard of care of the reasonable person involves in its application a subjective element. 

However, it must be remembered that the correct test is always reasonable care in all circumstances, not average care. The fact that most people behave in a certain way may be good evidence that the conduct is reasonable, but this is not necessarily the case. Although reasonableness is a very fluid concept, all of the evidence suggests that Binance.com did not foresee the fraud and disregarded even the most obvious dangers in this respect. 

Situations do tend to repeat themselves and it is advisable to examine previous outcomes to see how the standard of the reasonable person should be applied, and that lessons can be learnt from the errors of the past. 

Apropos of the fluidity of the concept of reasonableness, all Binance.com has done in this regard is set up a dichotomy of having or not having the legal obligation under consideration, however, that does not go one inch toward explaining why various regulatory authorities, has maintained that financial institutions can, and should, protect consumers using their systems, advanced technologies, and rich experience. 
Binance.com is obliged to take some action if it is sufficiently aware of a real possibility that a fraud may be being perpetuated. If you don't question its customers instructions or raise the possibility of a scam with the customer in these circumstances, it may be liable if the red flags indicate the customer is : particularly vulnerable, or if the possibility of fraud was serious or real, not just suspected. 
There are some recommendations to organizations for protecting customers from financial harm that might occur as a result of fraud or financial abuse ; and gives guidance on how to recognize customers who might be at risk, how to assess the potential risks to the individual and how to take the necessary actions to prevent or minimize financial harm. 
These recommendations are established as a general principle, the organization should deliver a service that : 1 ) Takes a proactive approach to minimizing risks, impact and incidences of financial harm and it sets out systems and tools for the prevention and detection of fraud and financial abuse. As a general point, it says organizations should ensure that all systems are developed using technologies and methodologies that are effective in the prevention of fraud and financial abuse, through authorized and unauthorized payments, thereby minimizing the risk of financial harm to customers. As regards to the detection of fraud and financial abuse, it says the organization : A ) should have measures in place across all payment channels and products to detect suspicious transactions or activities that might indicate fraud or financial abuse. It then lists the following examples of suspicious activity on customer accounts : a. multiple cheque books; b. sudden increased spending ; c. transfers to other accounts ; d. multiple password attempts ; e. logins from new devices, multiple geographical locations ; f. sudden changes to the operation of the account ; Unusual transactions are transactions whose amount, characteristics and frequency bear no relation to the economic activity of the customer, exceed normal market parameters or have no apparent legal justification. 
g. a withdrawal or payment for a large amount ; h. a payment or series of payments to a new payee ; i. financial activity that matches a known method of fraud or financial abuse. 
B ) organizations should have a process in place to ensure that staff make contact with the customer to verify the financial activity, challenge its authenticity, explain the nature of the suspected or detected fraud and discuss an appropriate plan of action. 
I am deeply convinced that the disastrous results that I have previously elaborated upon will continue to ensue if no responsibility is adopted by Binance.com in relation to this matter. I have also thoroughly detailed why they can not simply dismiss this problem by strictly adhering to legal technicalities which, after careful reflection, struck me as being nothing more than self-interest. Indeed, it seems to me utterly unfair to disregard fragile, sensitive, and vulnerable consumers who are afflicted by such allegedly malevolent acts, thereby keeping an unjust status-quo that is corrupting our society at its core. 

Conclusion : Based on my analysis, and as confirmed by various authorities concerned with such matters, there is abundant evidence that forward-thinking financial institutions and cryptocurrency trading platforms ought to take reasonable steps to forestall fraud, or at least mitigate its risk by using an effective risk management system, demonstrating their undisputed ability to responsibly and pre-emptively respond to questionable transactions in the digital arena. The use of such systems, largely based on newly adopted technologies aimed at effectively navigating the evolving threat landscape, is only one of several possible endeavors undertaken in this connection, alongside the application of past knowledge and experience related to popular fraudulent practices. 

Binance.coms non-observance of the fundamental principles of justice that is, to completely overlook and not even remotely try to mitigate the suffering of vulnerable consumers is inexcusable given the size of the establishment and the vast resources at its disposal as the direct result of the patronage of clients like myself. 

In summary, I respectively ask your organization to consider my points, given your personal and companywide obligation to provide a fair and reasonable investigation into the complaint. 

I look forward to your input and would gladly cooperate to reach a fair and reasonable outcome. 

Thank you.

I am writing to report a serious case of fraud and request HSBC to take responsibility for failing to prevent a suspicious, high-risk transaction. In XX/XX/year>, I was manipulated by scammers into making cryptocurrency-related payments. Following their instructions, I transferred over {$70000.00} USD from my HSBC account to a XXXX XXXX, which was later sent to fraudulent wallet addresses. 

I am a long-time HSBC customer . Since opening my account, I have never made any cryptocurrency-related or large third-party transfers like this. Despite this being a highly unusual transaction for my account history, HSBC issued no warning, no fraud alert, and no verification step before allowing the transfer to go through. 

After realizing I had been scammed, I reported the case to law enforcement. A formal investigation is now being conducted by the XXXX Police Department. I presented all related documentation to HSBC, including the police report and blockchain tracing information. However, HSBC has refused to accept any responsibility, stating that the transaction was authorized simply because I initiated it.

This ignores the fact that I was under false pretenses and psychological manipulation, which is a recognized form of fraud. HSBC failed to protect me as a customer by not recognizing an abnormal transaction and not providing any fraud prevention measures. 

I respectfully request that HSBC reconsiders this case and provides a partial reimbursement, along with a formal acknowledgment of their failure to act on an obvious red flag.

Product: Debt collection
Issue: Attempts to collect debt not owed
Consumer complaint narrative:
I received two emails, out of the blue, claiming to be from Resurgent regarding debt collection. None of the information in those emails was anything I recognized, including the last four digits of the " account '' in question or the fact that that original creditor was XXXX XXXX - of whom I've been a client in good standing for many years. 

I did not, and will not, click on any links in the email. 

Instead, I called XXXX XXXX first. They couldn't find anything in the records of my owing any money. I am diligent about bill paying and have a strong, clean credit report ( which I confirmed after receiving the Resurgent emails ). 

However, I know identify theft can create these issues so I called Resurgent to see if I can find out more. The first thing they asked for was my social security number which I refused to provide. I gave them the " Resurgent ID '' but they then pushed for more personal information in order to " verify '' me. All I wanted was an official USPS communication in order for me to 1. believe this is a real situation and not a scam and 2. understand the details behind this. But since I wouldn't share more and they wouldn't send any letters without verification, we reached an impasse and I had to hang up without resolution. I am dubious about his organization- I have no reason to believe I owe money but I do have reason to believe there could be nefarious behavior happening. 

I could use some help to find out how to verify all this and make sure there's not a real issue brewing for me. I always pay what I owe- but I won't be taken for a fool. Thank you for any guidance.

Product: Student loan
Issue: Dealing with your lender or servicer
Sub-issue: Trouble with how payments are being handled
Consumer complaint narrative: In either XXXX or XXXX of XXXX, the Federal government paused student loan repayments. Since that time, I have received no communication from EdFinancial regarding my loans or repayment status. Then, on XX/XX/XXXX, I received a series of letters in the mail ; one provided a statement, another a notification of a failed auto-payment and a third stating that my account was over 120 days late. I had no clue what these letters were and was shocked. I went to the link that the letters directed, and realized this was for EdFinancial and it wasn't a scam, seeking payment. When I logged into the account, I saw through the payment history, that they'd been attempting auto-debits since XX/XX/XXXX to an account that I had closed in XXXX. No notification had ever been sent to me to state that loans were going back into repayment. That aside, no notification has ever been sent to me to indicate that they'd been unsuccessfully attempting payment for the last 18 months. Only on XX/XX/XXXX had I received notice. I paid the late payment through my new checking account with another institution. When I spoke with an agent, she informed me that I was on electronic notification delivery. That said, not once had EdFinancial e-mailed me to say that they were attempting payment and payment had failed, or that they were even going into repayment status. Those messages sat in the EdFinancial inbox and no e-mail had ever been sent to my e-mail, which was on file with them, to indicate messages were waiting for me to read. Further, they had not sent me any letters in the mail either. They have now reported me 120 days late, even though I'm current and made myself current as soon as they actually notified me. This is incredibly deceptive and I made the late payment but if I'd been sent letter previously, as they finally did on XX/XX/XXXX, I would have addressed this before it ever went into a late status. They collected interest on me for loans that I could have already have paid off by now, if they'd only been responsible enough to reach out to me. I have received no e-mail or written letter from them in nearly 5 years and now they report me late without allowing me the opportunity to pay.

Product: Checking or savings account
Consumer complaint narrative:
Complaint Regarding Poor Service with Insulting Behaviors at Bank of America XXXX MA Branch I am writing to formally lodge a complaint about the unsatisfactory service with insulting behaviors I have recently experienced at XXXX, MA Bank of American branch located at XXXXXXXX XXXX XXXXXXXX As a long-standing customer of BOA, I have always valued the quality of service provided. However, my recent interactions have been disappointing and unacceptable. 

On the afternoon of XX/XX/XXXX, I visited the branch to withdraw {$8200.00} from my checking account. During this visit, I encountered several issues that I found to be highly unprofessional : Unprofessional Behavior : - When I requested to withdraw money from my own checking account, the representative requested to show my driver 's license and immediately asked me what I intended to use the money for. I have never been asked such privacy-related questions at any other banks where I hold accounts. There is no policy from BOA that requires customers to disclose how they intend to use their withdrawn funds under {$10000.00} ( for IRS purposes ). I found this question unexpected and intrusive. When I expressed that I considered this information private and did not wish to disclose it, it made me feel very uncomfortable. 

Misleading and Unclear Instructions with Insulting Behaviors : - Subsequently, the manager, XXXX XXXX, insisted that I sign a document titled Lets Talk About Ways to Help Protect Your Money in order to withdraw cash from my own account, and asked me to read through it without clearly explaining its purpose. He stated that this was a policy for withdrawing large amounts of cash to prevent bank fraud. This made me feel uncomfortable, offended, and suspected of fraudulent activity. When I inquired about when this policy started and the specific limit of cash withdrawals that required such paperwork, he could not provide a clear and direct answer. Upon quickly scanning the document, I realized it was merely informational content about preventing scams and fraud, with no signature field requiring my endorsement. When I asked why he was asking for my signature, he stated this was the bank policy ( which I couldn't find anyway from BOA site and documentation ) that his manager, XXXX XXXX XXXX, had instructed him to do so. And he has observed such fraud at the branch with 4-5 customers on a daily basis. 

When I expressed that I was not willing to sign such paperwork and that if this was required every time I withdrew money, I would close my account, he responded that he could immediately close my account and issue a check. I was shocked by this response and how BOA treats its customers with such disregard and insulting behaviors. If I did not have direct deposits and other account payments linked to my BOA account, I would have closed all my accounts with your bank. 

This experience has caused me considerable inconvenience and has shaken my confidence in the quality of service that BOA prides itself on. 

As a valued customer, I believe I deserve better treatment and prompt, professional service. I also question the bank 's practice of forcing customers to sign such informational paperwork, which could lead to legal issues. I request that immediate steps for the BOA be taken to address these issues and improve the service quality at this branch. Specifically, I would appreciate : 1.A thorough investigation into this matter and appropriate action against the staff involved and provide the statistics regarding the number of customers has been forced to give a signature on such paperwork. 
2.A formal apology for the inconvenience caused. 
3.Clear communication about the measures being implemented to prevent such issues in the future. 

I hope to see a swift and satisfactory resolution to this matter. I trust that you will handle my complaint with the seriousness it deserves. 

Thank you for your attention to this matter. Please see attachment for the paperwork that I was forced to sign. 

Thank you!

On XX/XX/year> my Capitol ONe credit card ending XXXX was locked due to my failed attempts changing password online. I called them ( Fraud Line ) on XX/XX/year>, they requested I send them copies of documents in the mail ( Drivers licence and something else? ). I told them I was not comfortable sending them XXXX, and quite honestly had never been required ever to reset a password with any acccount before ( Banks, Credit Cards, etc. ). 

I think even with them in the past, I said I understand this is an attempt to safeguard against hackers, but there's many other most common means to validate who I am. Send code to my phone, ask life questions etc. After lengthy conversation with no change on their request, I twice asked them to close the account. The person on the line repeated my request, confirmed it. Finally I hung up after not being requested anything else. No further charges were allowed due to the Credit Cards locked status, I paid in full the remaining balance on that next statement mailed to me. Then on or about 2 months later they added an annual charge of {$59.00}. I returned this bill payment with notes on the bill there should be no charges as the card was supposed to be cancelled two months earlier. I heard nothing back so assumed this had been done. Then I received the next month with penalty charges. I followed up after receiving a letter from them dated XX/XX/year> reminding me of past due balance and to write them if in dispute. I did, this time I sent a certified letter ( with receipt requested ) on XX/XX/year> explaining in detail what transpired with the bill and note of nothing should be due. This was signed for by CC company on XX/XX/year>. 

I received another bill, so called them again. I again spoke to the Fraud line where I was always transferred to even when calling the Customer Service line. The fraud line is where I initially requested the account closed. I called again on XX/XX/scrub>XXXX  and spoke to XXXX, she said she did see in their notes of XX/XX/year> that I requested the account closed, but the account is not closed. She asked if I wanted to close it, I said yes out of frustration and stated numerous times this should have been closed on XX/XX/year>, I said there should be no balance as there was not until new charges ( yearly ) fee was added two months after account should have been closed and no charges had been incurred by me. Now I see they've reported me to Credit Burueau with 30 day late payment. I am the card holder but also had given my two daughters this card and my wife. Turns out the all have this 30 day late on their credit. Even though I wholly dispute any of these charges on a card that should have been cancelled, I'm the card holder/payer, not sure why my daughters would have a late payment on their credit report too? 

In summary, they should have closed this when I asked on XX/XX/year>, I had paid all outstanding balances then they added to the balance of XXXX a yearly fee two months later and now subsquent late fees and hit on my ( and my families ) credit report. I was an excellent customer, but their refusal to close the card as requested ( and they have in their files ) seems like a scam to continue charges
